content.body: DNA replication copies the genetic code before cell division. Gene expression converts the DNA code into proteins through transcription (DNA→mRNA) and translation (mRNA→protein).

Central Dogma: DNA → RNA → Protein

Transcription: DNA template → mRNA (in nucleus)
Translation: mRNA → protein (at ribosome)

Codon: 3 mRNA bases = 1 amino acid
64 codons code for 20 amino acids (genetic code is degenerate)

content.title: Transcription Example
content.body:
DNA template: 3'-TAC-CGT-AAA-5'
mRNA produced: 5'-AUG-GCA-UUU-3'
(A pairs with U in RNA; T pairs with A; G pairs with C)
AUG = Start codon (Met)
GCA = Alanine
UUU = Phenylalanine

questions.question: Transcription produces:
questions.answer: mRNA from DNA
questions.explanation: Transcription reads the DNA template to produce messenger RNA (mRNA)

questions.question: Translation occurs at the:
questions.answer: Ribosome
questions.explanation: Ribosomes read mRNA codons and assemble amino acids into proteins

questions.question: How many mRNA bases make one codon?
questions.explanation: A codon is a triplet of 3 RNA bases that codes for one amino acid

questions.question: The start codon that begins translation is:
questions.answer: AUG
questions.explanation: AUG is the universal start codon, coding for methionine

questions.question: DNA replication is called semi-conservative because:
options: Only half the DNA is copied
Each new double helix has one old and one new strand
It happens in half the time
questions.answer: Each new double helix has one old and one new strand
questions.explanation: Each new DNA molecule retains one original strand — semi-conservative replication

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

content.body: Gene expression is the process by which information in DNA is used to produce functional gene products (proteins or RNA). Not all genes are expressed at all times — gene regulation determines when and where genes are active.

Central Dogma:
DNA → (transcription) → mRNA → (translation) → Protein

Transcription (nucleus):
RNA polymerase reads DNA template (3'→5') and builds mRNA (5'→3')
Pre-mRNA: introns spliced out, exons joined → mature mRNA

Translation (ribosome):
mRNA codons (3 bases) → tRNA anticodons bring amino acids
Start codon AUG → polypeptide → stop codon (UAA, UAG, UGA)

content.title: Gene Regulation — Lac Operon (Prokaryotes)
content.body:
E. coli regulates lactose metabolism via the lac operon:
No lactose present: repressor binds operator → genes OFF
Lactose present: lactose binds repressor → repressor leaves operator → genes ON (β-galactosidase made)

Eukaryotic regulation is more complex:
• Transcription factors bind promoters/enhancers
• Chromatin remodelling (histone acetylation)
• DNA methylation silences genes

questions.question: The central dogma of molecular biology states...
questions.answer: DNA → RNA → Protein
questions.explanation: The central dogma: DNA is transcribed into RNA, which is translated into protein.

questions.question: Which enzyme carries out transcription?
questions.answer: RNA polymerase
questions.explanation: RNA polymerase reads the DNA template and synthesises complementary mRNA.

questions.question: Introns are...
questions.answer: Non-coding sequences removed from pre-mRNA
questions.explanation: Introns (intervening sequences) are spliced out during RNA processing. Only exons appear in the mature mRNA.

questions.question: A codon consists of how many bases?
questions.explanation: Each codon is a triplet of bases in mRNA that codes for a specific amino acid (or start/stop signal).

questions.question: The start codon is...
questions.answer: AUG
questions.explanation: AUG codes for methionine and is the universal start codon. Translation always begins at AUG.

questions.question: In the lac operon, lactose acts as an...
questions.answer: Inducer
questions.explanation: Lactose (converted to allolactose) binds the repressor protein, inactivating it — inducing (switching on) gene transcription.

questions.question: Transcription factors in eukaryotes bind to...
options: Promoter and enhancer regions of DNA
questions.answer: Promoter and enhancer regions of DNA
questions.explanation: Transcription factors are proteins that bind regulatory DNA sequences to activate or repress gene transcription.

questions.question: DNA methylation generally...
questions.answer: Silences genes
questions.explanation: Adding methyl groups to cytosine residues in DNA (especially at CpG islands in promoters) typically silences gene expression.

questions.question: Which molecule carries amino acids to the ribosome?
questions.answer: tRNA
questions.explanation: Transfer RNA (tRNA) has an anticodon that matches mRNA codons and carries the corresponding amino acid to the ribosome.

questions.question: Epigenetic changes affect gene expression by...
options: Modifying chromatin structure without changing DNA sequence
questions.answer: Modifying chromatin structure without changing DNA sequence
questions.explanation: Epigenetics: heritable changes in gene expression that don't involve DNA sequence changes (e.g. methylation, histone modification).

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy

questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Genetic engineering involves manipulating an organism's DNA to achieve desired outcomes. Biotechnology applies biological systems to industrial, medical, and agricultural applications.

Steps in genetic engineering:
1. Identify and isolate the gene of interest
   - Use restriction enzymes to cut DNA at specific sites
   - Reverse transcriptase: mRNA → cDNA
2. Insert gene into vector (plasmid or virus)
   - Restriction enzyme creates sticky ends
   - DNA ligase joins gene to vector
3. Transform vector into host cell
4. Select transformed cells
5. Scale up production

content.title: Applications of Genetic Engineering
content.body:
Insulin production: human insulin gene → E. coli → produces human insulin
Golden Rice: β-carotene (vitamin A) genes inserted → rice grains make vitamin A → combat deficiency in developing world
Bt crops: Bacillus thuringiensis toxin gene → insect-resistant crops → less pesticide use
Gene therapy: replace faulty gene in patients (e.g. SCID — 'bubble boy' disease)

content.title: PCR — Polymerase Chain Reaction
content.body:
Amplifies tiny amounts of DNA:
1. Denaturation (95°C): separate DNA strands
2. Annealing (~60°C): primers bind to target sequences
3. Extension (72°C): Taq polymerase copies DNA

Cycle 30 times: 2³⁰ = ~10⁹ copies from one molecule

Applications: forensic DNA profiling, diagnosing pathogens, studying ancient DNA, COVID-19 testing

questions.question: Restriction enzymes cut DNA at
questions.answer: specific recognition sequences (palindromes)
questions.explanation: Restriction endonucleases recognise specific short palindromic sequences (e.g. GAATTC) and cut there — molecular 'scissors'.

questions.question: Sticky ends are
options: short single-stranded DNA overhangs created by staggered cuts — allow complementary fragments to join
questions.answer: short single-stranded DNA overhangs created by staggered cuts — allow complementary fragments to join
questions.explanation: Staggered restriction cuts leave short unpaired overhangs. Complementary sticky ends from gene and vector pair and are joined by ligase.

questions.question: DNA ligase in genetic engineering
options: joins DNA fragments — seals gaps in sugar-phosphate backbone
questions.answer: joins DNA fragments — seals gaps in sugar-phosphate backbone
questions.explanation: Ligase: the 'glue' that permanently joins the gene insert to the vector by forming phosphodiester bonds.

questions.question: Plasmids are used as vectors because
options: they self-replicate in bacteria, can carry inserted genes and are easily transferred
questions.answer: they self-replicate in bacteria, can carry inserted genes and are easily transferred
questions.explanation: Plasmids: small circular bacterial DNA. Self-replicate independently, easily taken up by bacteria, can carry foreign genes.

questions.question: Reverse transcriptase makes
questions.answer: DNA from RNA template
questions.explanation: Reverse transcriptase (from retroviruses) converts mRNA → cDNA. Used to get intron-free gene copies from mRNA.

questions.question: In PCR, Taq polymerase is used because
options: it is heat-stable, surviving the 95°C denaturation step
questions.answer: it is heat-stable, surviving the 95°C denaturation step
questions.explanation: Taq polymerase from Thermus aquaticus (hot spring bacterium) remains active at high temperatures needed in PCR.

questions.question: Golden Rice was created to
options: provide vitamin A (β-carotene) to reduce deficiency in rice-eating populations
questions.answer: provide vitamin A (β-carotene) to reduce deficiency in rice-eating populations
questions.explanation: Vitamin A deficiency causes blindness in millions. Golden Rice has inserted genes for β-carotene synthesis in the endosperm.

questions.question: Gene therapy aims to
options: treat inherited genetic diseases by inserting functional copies of faulty genes
questions.answer: treat inherited genetic diseases by inserting functional copies of faulty genes
questions.explanation: Gene therapy: deliver correct copies of mutant genes into patient's cells (using viral vectors) to restore normal function.

questions.question: Gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments by
options: size — smaller fragments travel further through gel in electric field
questions.answer: size — smaller fragments travel further through gel in electric field
questions.explanation: DNA is negatively charged, moves toward positive electrode. Smaller fragments move faster through gel matrix → different band positions.

questions.question: DNA profiling (fingerprinting) uses
options: comparison of unique DNA fragment patterns (STR repeats) to identify individuals
questions.answer: comparison of unique DNA fragment patterns (STR repeats) to identify individuals
questions.explanation: DNA profiling: PCR amplifies specific STR (short tandem repeat) regions, electrophoresis separates them → unique pattern per individual.

questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Advanced Genetics. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Advanced Genetics concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Advanced Genetics builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Advanced Genetics. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Advanced Genetics. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Advanced Genetics include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Advanced Genetics. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Advanced Genetics. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Advanced Genetics concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Advanced Genetics builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Advanced Genetics. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Advanced Genetics. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Advanced Genetics include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Advanced Genetics. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
